text: The surgeon addresses a symptomatic right inguinal hernia in a young male patient. The incarcerated omentum is visible, adherent to the inguinal canal, and is removed. The surgeon then proceeds with the repair, entering the preperitoneal plane from the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the medial umbilical ligaments.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, adjusting the tissue to elevate the fat and lower the bladder, followed by lateral dissection in the preperitoneal plane.

The surgeon creates space for mesh placement by leaving some fat on the abdominal wall and identifying the cord structures near the anal ring. The cord structures are swept downwards as the surgeon moves out of the inner-warf metal.

The surgeon clears fat over the femoral space, ensuring no hernia is present, and proceeds to the obturator space. The vas and cord structures are cleared, and the surgeon prioritizes these structures to create space for mesh placement, moving inferiorly and laterally. The surgeon points out the four hernia locations within the inguinal canal.

The surgeon confirms the absence of a cord lipoma and proceeds to place the mesh over the inguinal canal. The mesh is positioned to cover the medial pubic area (MPO) effectively. Sutures are then used to close the flap over the mesh.
The surgeon uses a suture to close a small hole in the hernia sac, ensuring the flap remains closed. An angiocath is used to evacuate gas from the peritoneal flap.
